scope: Encoding subject-matter expertise into derived features. Explains how business logic, medical indices, and financial formulas are translated into mathematical features.
key_concepts: Domain-driven features are explicit mathematical formulations of human expertise and industry-specific logic.
They bypass the need for a model to 'learn' known scientific or business facts from scratch, saving training time and improving accuracy.
These features often take the form of standard indices (e.g., BMI, credit scores) or specialized business rules.
They represent the purest form of human-designed representations, injecting prior knowledge directly into the dataset.

definitions.definition: A feature constructed using specific subject-matter expertise or established industry formulas rather than generic mathematical operations.

examples.description: Calculating Body Mass Index (BMI) from height and weight in a medical dataset.
Computing the 'loan-to-value' ratio in a mortgage dataset using established financial regulations.

relations.builds_toward: The synthesis in Chapter 17 on when to use designed vs learned representations.

exclusions: Deep dive into the specific medical or financial theory behind the formulas.
